Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty loose. I'd usually carve out chunks of time during the week, maybe 27 hours total, but it wasn't like I had a strict schedule. I'm 27, so I've got other stuff going on in life, and I'd often study when I felt like it or when something was due. I learn best by listening, so I'd play lecture recordings while doing other things around the house.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was happening. If a deadline was coming up, I'd cram more hours in, but otherwise I'd drift a bit. Some weeks I was really on it, other weeks not so much. I think that's why my attendance wasn't great—I showed up when I felt it mattered, not necessarily every single time.
Q3: Usually it was either a notification about something due or just wanting to check if there was new content posted. Sometimes I'd log in because I was bored and figured I should probably do something productive. The discussion boards were actually kind of interesting, so occasionally I'd pop in just to see what people were talking about.
Q4: Yeah, there was a stretch where logging in became pretty automatic for me. I'd check it in the morning with my coffee, kind of like checking social media. It happened because I got into the rhythm of the peer support stuff—having people to interact with made it feel less like a chore and more like a community thing.
Q5: The video lectures were my lifeline since I'm an auditory learner. I watched a ton of them—like, almost two hours' worth that I can remember clearly. The discussion posts were also useful because hearing other people's perspectives helped things click for me. When peers explained concepts their way, it often made more sense than the formal materials.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the reading materials and some of the optional quizzes. They just didn't grab me the way audio content did. I'd skim PDFs but rarely read them thoroughly. If something required me to sit and read silently for a long time, I'd put it off or just not do it at all.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it was either directly tied to my grade or if it genuinely helped me understand something I was confused about. Also, if it involved interacting with other students, I was more likely to engage. I need that human element to stay interested, otherwise it all feels very sterile.
Q8: Optional activities felt like suggestions I could ignore, honestly. Required ones had that pressure attached, so I'd do them even if I wasn't feeling it. But sometimes the optional stuff was actually more interesting—it's just that without the deadline, I'd tell myself I'd get to it later and then never did.
Q9: Oh yeah, all the time. I'd download the audio files and listen to them on my phone while commuting or doing chores. I also had a notebook where I'd jot down things I heard in lectures. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the screen, just processing what I'd listened to earlier.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was working through concepts in my head or discussing them with a friend from class over coffee, and none of that would show up anywhere. I'd replay lectures in my mind or talk through problems out loud. That's how I process things, but the LMS wouldn't know any of that.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download a lecture once, then listen to it multiple times offline over the next few days. The LMS would only see that one download, but I might have engaged with that material four or five times. Same with some of the discussion threads—I'd screenshot interesting points and refer back to them later.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when everything piled up. I had some personal stuff going on and work responsibilities that competed for my attention. My attendance dropped and I felt disconnected for a while. The peer support helped pull me back in, but there were weeks where I was just going through the motions.
Q13: Fatigue was a big one for me. Being 27 and juggling multiple responsibilities, I'd sometimes log in exhausted and just not have the mental energy to really engage. I'd click through things without absorbing much. Competing responsibilities definitely made me prioritize what was urgent over what was important for learning.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd have videos playing but I was mentally somewhere else. I'd realize I'd been staring at the screen for twenty minutes and couldn't tell you what was said. Those sessions felt like wasted time, but they'd still show up as engagement in the system.
Q15: I procrastinated on the written assignments even though I knew they were important. Writing isn't my strong suit since I process things better through listening and talking. I'd put them off until the last minute, which is probably why my assignment completion wasn't perfect even though my scores were good when I did submit.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my engine. Without them, I'd drift. When something was due, I'd suddenly become very active on the LMS, watching videos I'd been putting off, checking requirements, posting in discussions. My activity probably looked very spiky—quiet periods followed by intense bursts right before due dates.
Q17: When I got good feedback, it motivated me to engage more with similar activities. But honestly, feedback sometimes came too late to really change my approach for the next thing. The peer feedback was actually more influential because it was more immediate and felt more personal than instructor comments.
Q18: The course structure was okay, but I thrived more when there were collaborative elements. The peer support aspect really helped me stay connected. When modules were very independent and self-directed, I'd lose steam. I needed that social accountability to keep showing up.
Q19: Having video content made engagement much easier for me as an auditory learner. What made it harder was when everything was text-heavy or when deadlines bunched up together. Also, the discussion forums were great, but sometimes the prompts felt forced rather than organic, which made participating feel like a chore.
Q20: I think that could be someone who's genuinely trying to learn and exploring materials but struggling with the actual output part. Maybe they're anxious about submitting work or they're perfectionists who never feel ready. Or they could be like me sometimes—engaged with consuming content but procrastinating on producing anything.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. My exam score wasn't amazing, but I passed, and a lot of my real studying happened offline. Someone could be listening to downloaded lectures, studying from notes, or learning through conversations with classmates, and none of that would show up in the logs.
Q22: I think logs can show patterns—like when you're active, how much content you access, whether you participate in discussions. But they miss the quality of engagement entirely. They can't tell if you were actually thinking deeply or just clicking through. They also miss all the offline learning that happens.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actually mentally present and caring about what I'm learning. It's not just showing up or clicking buttons—it's when I'm curious, when I'm connecting ideas, when I want to discuss things with others. It's that feeling of being invested rather than just going through motions to get a grade.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I relied on listening to content repeatedly offline, or how meaningful the peer conversations were to my understanding. It wouldn't capture that even though I seemed disengaged sometimes, I was actually processing things in my own way. My happiness and resilience kept me going even when the data might have suggested I was struggling.
